_readme: Use entries[] as full word records. Merge each object into Database.json by id (replace matching element).

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin confluentia, from confluere 'flow together'.
entries.exampleSentence: A confluence of different factors made tonight the perfect night.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "flu" (flow) = rivers (or ideas) flowing together into one.

entries.definition: One who behaves the same as others
entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from conform + -ist. Originally applied to someone conforming to the practices of the Church of England.
entries.exampleSentence: Julian was such a conformist that he had to wait and see if his friends would do something before he would commit.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "form" = taking the exact same form or shape as the rest of society.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French confondre, from Latin confundere 'pour together, mix up'.
entries.exampleSentence: MacGuyver confounded the policemen pursuing him by covering his tracks.

memoryHook.text: Dumbfounded - you are so thoroughly confused you are left dumbfounded.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French congeler, from Latin congelare, from con- 'together' + gelare 'to freeze'.
entries.exampleSentence: The sauce had congealed into a thick paste.

memoryHook.text: Jell-O - it cools down and thickens into a solid mass, just like Jell-O.

entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin con- 'together' + genialis 'festive'.
entries.exampleSentence: His congenial manner made him popular wherever he went.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "gen" (nature) = sharing the same nature; highly agreeable and friendly.

entries.definition: A gathering of people, especially for religious services
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin congregatio(n-), from congregare 'collect into a flock'.
entries.exampleSentence: The priest told the congregation that he would be retiring.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "greg" (flock) = a flock of people gathering together.

entries.definition: The quality of being in agreement
entries.etymology: Late 15th century: from Old French congruite or late Latin congruitas, from congruus 'agreeing'.
entries.exampleSentence: Bill and Veronica achieved a perfect congruity of opinion.

memoryHook.text: Geometry - just like congruent triangles, everything fits perfectly together in agreement.

entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from French conniver or Latin connivere 'shut the eyes (to)'.
entries.exampleSentence: She connived to get me to give up my vacation plans.

memoryHook.text: Knives - secretly plotting behind someone's back with knives.

entries.definition: To dedicate something to a holy purpose
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consecrat- 'dedicated, devoted as sacred', from the verb consecrare.
entries.exampleSentence: Arvin consecrated his spare bedroom as a shrine to Christina.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "sacr" (sacred) = to officially declare something holy.

entries.etymology: Mid 19th century: from Latin, 'agreement', from consens- 'agreed', from the verb consentire.
entries.exampleSentence: The jury was able to reach a consensus only after days of deliberation.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "sens" (feel) = everyone in the room feeling the same way; a general agreement.

entries.definition: To give something over to another’s care
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from French consigner or Latin consignare 'mark with a seal'.
entries.exampleSentence: Unwillingly, he consigned his mother to a nursing home.

memoryHook.text: Sign - you sign the paperwork to formally hand the goods over to someone else's care.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consolatio(n-), from the verb consolari 'to comfort'.
entries.exampleSentence: Darren found Alexandra’s presence to be a consolation for his suffering.

memoryHook.text: Console - giving a sad gamer a new console to comfort them after a major loss.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin consonant- 'sounding together', from the verb consonare.
entries.exampleSentence: The singers’ consonant voices were beautiful.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "son" (sound) = sounding together in perfect harmony.

entries.etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin constituent- 'establishing, appointing', from the verb constituere.
entries.exampleSentence: The most important constituent of her perfume is something called ambergris.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French constraindre, from Latin constringere 'bind tightly together'.
entries.exampleSentence: His belief in nonviolence constrained him from taking revenge on his attackers.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin construere 'build up, assemble', from con- 'together' + struere 'build'.
entries.exampleSentence: He construed her throwing his clothes out the window as a signal that she wanted him to leave.

memoryHook.text: Construct - you carefully construct the meaning of a complex sentence in your mind.

entries.definition: To complete a deal; to complete a marriage ceremony.
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consummat- 'brought to completion', from the verb consummare.
entries.exampleSentence: Erica and Donald consummated their agreement in the executive boardroom.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "summa" (highest) = brought to the absolute highest, most complete level of skill.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consumptio(n-), from the verb consumere 'use up'.
entries.exampleSentence: Consumption of intoxicating beverages is not permitted on these premises.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin contemporaneus (from con- 'together with' + tempus, tempor- 'time') + -ous.
entries.exampleSentence: Though her novels do not feature the themes of Romanticism, Jane Austen’s work was contemporaneous with that of Wordsworth and Byron.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "tempor" (time) = two events happening at the exact same time.

entries.definition: Having a tendency to quarrel or dispute
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French contentieux, from Latin contentiosus, from content- 'striven'.
entries.exampleSentence: George’s contentious personality made him unpopular with his classmates.

memoryHook.text: Contender - a boxer who loves to fight and argue.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from late Latin contravenire, from Latin contra 'against' + venire 'come'.
entries.exampleSentence: Edwidge contravened his landlady’s rule against overnight guests.

memoryHook.text: "contra-" (against) + "venire" (come) = to come against a direct rule or law.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French contrit, from Latin contritus, past participle of conterere 'grind down'.
entries.exampleSentence: Blake’s contrite behavior made it impossible to stay angry at him.

memoryHook.text: From Latin contritus (ground to pieces). Your heart is crushed and ground to pieces by the guilt of what you've done.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin contusio(n-), from the verb contundere 'to pound, bruise'.
entries.exampleSentence: The contusions on his face suggested he’d been in a fight.

memoryHook.text: Confusion - getting hit in the head so hard it leaves a massive bruise and causes confusion.

entries.etymology: Late 16th century: a word of unknown origin, originally an Oxford University slang term for a pedant.
entries.exampleSentence: Interpreting Jane’s behavior was a constant conundrum.

memoryHook.text: Nun drum - a nun playing a heavy metal drum kit is a confusing, puzzling situation to figure out.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin convenire 'assemble, agree, fit', from con- 'together' + venire 'come'.
entries.exampleSentence: Jason convened his entire extended family for a discussion.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "venire" (come) = to officially come together for a meeting.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin conventio(n-) 'meeting, covenant', from the verb convenire.

senses.exampleSentence: The hotel was full because of the cattleranchers’ convention.
The cattle-ranchers have a convention that you take off your boots before entering their houses.

entries.exampleSentence: The hotel was full because of the cattleranchers’ convention.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin convivialis, from convivium 'a feast', from con- 'with' + vivere 'live'.
entries.exampleSentence: The restaurant’s convivial atmosphere put me immediately at ease.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "viv" (life) = full of life, highly festive, and sociable.

entries.etymology: Late 18th century: past participle of convolute, from Latin convolutus 'rolled together'.
entries.exampleSentence: Grace’s story was so convoluted that I couldn’t follow it.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "volvere" (roll) = rolled and twisted together so many times it becomes impossibly complicated.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French copieux or Latin copiosus, from copia 'plenty'.
entries.exampleSentence: Copious amounts of Snapple were imbibed in the cafeteria.

memoryHook.text: Copy us - the printer made so many copies you now have an abundant supply.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from medieval Latin cordialis, from Latin cor, cord- 'heart'.
entries.exampleSentence: His cordial greeting melted my anger at once.

memoryHook.text: "cord" (heart) = acting in a warm, polite, and heartfelt way.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin coronatio(n-), from coronare 'to crown'.
entries.exampleSentence: The new king’s coronation occurred the day after his father’s death.

memoryHook.text: "coron" (crown) = the official ceremony of giving someone the crown.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin corpulentia, from corpulentus 'fleshy', from corpus 'body'.
entries.exampleSentence: Henry’s corpulence did not make him any less attractive to his charming, svelte wife.

memoryHook.text: "corp" (body) = having a massive, excessively fat body.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin corroborat- 'strengthened', from the verb corroborare.
entries.exampleSentence: Luke’s seemingly outrageous claim was corroborated by witnesses.

memoryHook.text: "cor-" (together) + "robor" (strength) = to add strength to a witness's story by providing matching evidence.

entries.definition: Having the tendency to erode or eat away
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French corosif, -ive, from late Latin corrosivus, from Latin corrodere 'gnaw through'.
entries.exampleSentence: The effect of the chemical was highly corrosive.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Greek kosmopolitēs (from kosmos 'world' + politēs 'citizen') + -an.
entries.exampleSentence: Lloyd’s education and upbringing were cosmopolitan, so he felt right at home among the powerful and learned.

memoryHook.text: "cosm" (world) + "polit" (citizen) = a citizen of the world; highly sophisticated and well-traveled.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from counter- 'against' + act.
entries.exampleSentence: The antidote counteracted the effect of the poison.

entries.etymology: Late 18th century: from French, from medieval Latin colpus 'blow'.

senses.exampleSentence: Alexander pulled off an amazing coup when he got a date with Cynthia by purposely getting hit by her car.

senses.definition: The overthrow of a government and assumption of authority
senses.exampleSentence: In their coup attempt, the army officers stormed the Parliament and took all the legislators hostage.

memoryHook.text: Coop - the angry chickens violently overthrew the farmer to take control of the coop.

entries.exampleSentence: Alexander pulled off an amazing coup when he got a date with Cynthia by purposely getting hit by her car.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French cuveitier, based on Latin cupiditas 'cupidity'.
entries.exampleSentence: I coveted Moses’s house, wife, and car.

memoryHook.text: Cover it - you want your neighbor's new car so badly you throw a tarp over it to steal it.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French, past participle of covrir 'to cover'.
entries.exampleSentence: Nerwin waged a covert campaign against his enemies, while outwardly appearing to remain friendly.

memoryHook.text: Cover it - keeping the military operation completely covered up and secret.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French credulite or Latin credulitas, from credulus 'believing'.
entries.exampleSentence: His credulity made him an easy target for con men.

memoryHook.text: "cred" (believe) = an excessive readiness to believe things; extreme gullibility.

entries.definition: A steady increase in intensity or volume
entries.etymology: Late 18th century: Italian, present participle of crescere 'to grow', from Latin.
entries.exampleSentence: The crescendo of the brass instruments gave the piece a patriotic feel.

entries.definition: Standards by which something is judged
entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from Greek kritērion 'means of judging', from kritēs 'judge'.
entries.exampleSentence: Among Mrs. Fields’s criteria for good cookies are that they be moist and chewy.

entries.definition: The climax toward which something progresses
entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin culminatio(n-), from the verb culminare 'to crown'.
entries.exampleSentence: The culmination of the couple’s argument was the decision to divorce.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French coupable, from Latin culpabilis, from culpa 'fault, blame'.
entries.exampleSentence: He was culpable of the crime, and was sentenced to perform community service for 75 years.

memoryHook.text: "culp" (blame) = fully deserving of the blame.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin cultivat- 'prepared for crops', from the verb cultivare.
entries.exampleSentence: At the library, she cultivated her interest in spy novels.

entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin cumulatus 'heaped up' + -ive.
entries.exampleSentence: The cumulative effect of hours spent in the sun was a deep tan.

entries.etymology: Middle English: perhaps from Old Norse kunnandi 'knowledge', from kunna 'know'.
entries.exampleSentence: The general devised a cunning plan to surprise the enemy.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French cupidite or Latin cupiditas, from cupidus 'desirous', from cupere 'to desire'.
entries.exampleSentence: His cupidity made him enter the abandoned gold mine despite the obvious dangers.

memoryHook.text: Named after Cupid, the Roman god of desire. While Cupid is associated with love, the word evolved to mean an extreme, greedy desire for…

entries.definition: Brief to the point of being superficial
entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from French cursoire or late Latin cursorius 'of a runner', from Latin cursor 'runner'.
entries.exampleSentence: Late for the meeting, she cast a cursory glance at the agenda.

memoryHook.text: Cursor - moving your computer cursor quickly over the text without actually stopping to read it.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin curtus 'cut short, abridged'.
entries.exampleSentence: Her curt reply to my question made me realize that she was upset at me.

memoryHook.text: Cut - a spoken response so short and rude it feels like you've been cut off.

entries.etymology: Late 15th century: from older curtal, from French courtault, from court 'short'.
entries.exampleSentence: Since losing his job, he had to curtail his spending.

memoryHook.text: Cut tail - to cut the tail off a long meeting to shorten it.

entries.definition: Intimidating, causing one to lose courage
entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French danter, from Latin domare 'to tame'.
entries.exampleSentence: He kept delaying the daunting act of asking for a promotion.

memoryHook.text: Haunting - a ghost haunting your house is an incredibly intimidating and scary problem to deal with.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from dear + -th. Original sense was 'shortness of food'.
entries.exampleSentence: An eager reader, she was dismayed by the dearth of classic books at the library.

memoryHook.text: Dead earth - a dead patch of earth has a severe scarcity and lack of crops.

entries.etymology: Early 19th century: from French débâcle, from débâcler 'unbar', from dé- 'un-' + bâcler 'to bar'.
entries.exampleSentence: The elaborately designed fireworks show turned into a debacle when the fireworks started firing in random directions.

memoryHook.text: Da buckle - the buckle on the CEO's belt breaks during a keynote speech, causing a complete public disaster.

entries.definition: To lower the quality or esteem of something
entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from de- 'down' + base, probably on the pattern of abase.
entries.exampleSentence: The large raise that he gave himself debased his motives for running the charity.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down) + "base" = to lower the value, status, or quality of something.

entries.definition: To corrupt by means of sensual pleasures
entries.etymology: Late 16th century: from French débaucher 'turn away from one's duty'.
entries.exampleSentence: An endless amount of good wine and cheese debauched the traveler.

memoryHook.text: Da botch - you completely botched your life by engaging in extreme, corrupt partying.

entries.definition: To expose the falseness of something
entries.etymology: 1920s: from de- (expressing removal) + bunk.
entries.exampleSentence: He debunked her claim to be the world’s greatest chess player by defeating her in 18 consecutive matches.

memoryHook.text: De-bunk - kicking your brother out of the top bunk because his claim that he called dibs is entirely false.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin decorus 'becoming' + -ous.
entries.exampleSentence: The appreciative guest displayed decorous behavior toward his host.

memoryHook.text: Decorate - having excellent manners, perfectly presented like a beautifully decorated room.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from de- 'down' + cry, on the pattern of French décrier.
entries.exampleSentence: The kind video rental clerk decried the policy of charging customers late fees.

memoryHook.text: Cry - crying out publicly against a policy you hate.

entries.definition: To ruin or injure something’s appearance
entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French desfacier, from des- (expressing removal) + face 'face'.
entries.exampleSentence: The brothers used eggs and shaving cream to deface their neighbor’s mailbox.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down/remove) + "face" = to ruin the surface or outward appearance of something (like graffiti).

entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from medieval Latin diffamatorius, from diffamare 'publish a bad report'.
entries.exampleSentence: The defamatory gossip spreading about the actor made the public less willing to see the actor’s new movie.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (remove) + "fame" = words specifically designed to destroy someone's fame or reputation.

entries.definition: To postpone something; to yield to another’s wisdom
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French differer 'differ, defer', from Latin differre.
entries.exampleSentence: Ron deferred to Diane, the expert on musical instruments, when he was asked about buying a piano.

entries.etymology: Early 19th century: from deference + -ial.
entries.exampleSentence: His deferential attitude toward her made her more confident in her ability to run the company.

memoryHook.text: Reference - treating someone with such deep respect that you always use them as a reference.

entries.etymology: Middle English: alteration of defoul, from Old French defouler 'trample down', influenced by file 'to foul'.
entries.exampleSentence: She defiled the calm of the religious building by playing her banjo.

memoryHook.text: File - spilling black coffee all over a clean file, completely corrupting and dirtying it.

entries.etymology: Middle English: variant of daft, in the sense 'meek'.
entries.exampleSentence: Having worked in a bakery for many years, Marcus was a deft bread maker.

memoryHook.text: Theft - a pickpocket must be incredibly quick and skillful to pull off a theft.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin defunctus 'dead', past participle of defungi 'carry out, finish'.
entries.exampleSentence: They planned to turn the defunct schoolhouse into a community center.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (off) + "funct" (perform) = no longer functioning; dead or extinct.

entries.definition: To hand over responsibility for something
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin delegatus 'sent on a commission', from the verb delegare.
entries.exampleSentence: The dean delegated the task of finding a new professor to a special hiring committee.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: via medieval Latin from Greek dēlētērios 'noxious', from dēlētēr 'destroyer'.
entries.exampleSentence: She experienced the deleterious effects of running a marathon without stretching her muscles enough beforehand.

memoryHook.text: Delete - a computer virus that is so harmful it deletes all your hard drive files.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin deliberatus, past participle of deliberare 'consider carefully'.
entries.exampleSentence: Though Mary was quite upset, her actions to resolve the dispute were deliberate.

entries.definition: To describe, outline, shed light on
entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin delineat- 'outlined', from the verb delineare, from de- 'out, completely' + linea 'line'.
entries.exampleSentence: She neatly delineated her reasons for canceling the project’s funding.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (out) + "line" = to trace the exact lines or outline of a plan in detail.

entries.definition: A leader who appeals to a people’s prejudices
entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Greek dēmagōgos, from dēmos 'the people' + agōgos 'leading'.
entries.exampleSentence: The demagogue strengthened his hold over his people by blaming immigrants for the lack of jobs.

memoryHook.text: "dem" (people) + "agog" (leader) = a toxic political leader who manipulates the dark emotions of the people to gain power.

entries.definition: The marking of boundaries or categories
entries.etymology: Early 18th century: from Spanish demarcación, from demarcar 'mark the bounds of'.
entries.exampleSentence: Different cultures have different demarcations of good and evil.

memoryHook.text: The mark - setting the physical mark on the ground to define a clear boundary.

entries.definition: To lower the status or stature of something
entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from de- 'completely' + mean (in the sense 'inferior').
entries.exampleSentence: She refused to demean her secretary by making him order her lunch.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down) + "mean" = to put someone down and intentionally make them feel inferior.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: perhaps from Old French demoure, past participle of demourer 'remain'.
entries.exampleSentence: Though everyone else at the party was dancing and going crazy, she remained demure.

memoryHook.text: Murmur - a shy, modest girl who only speaks in a quiet murmur.

entries.definition: To belittle, diminish the opinion of
entries.etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin denigrat- 'blackened', from the verb denigrare, from de- 'completely' + niger 'black'.
entries.exampleSentence: The company decided that its advertisements would no longer denigrate the company’s competitors.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (completely) + "nigr" (black) = to heavily blacken someone's reputation with insults.

entries.etymology: Middle English: from Old French denoncier, from Latin denuntiare 'give official information'.
entries.exampleSentence: The senator denounced her opponent as a greedy politician.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down) + "nuntiare" (speak/announce) = to publicly announce your rejection of someone.

entries.definition: To feel or express sorrow, disapproval
entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from French déplorer or Italian deplorare, from Latin deplorare.
entries.exampleSentence: We all deplored the miserable working conditions in the factory.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: alteration of obsolete pravity, from Latin pravitas, from pravus 'crooked, perverse'.
entries.exampleSentence: Rumors of the ogre’s depravity made the children afraid to enter the forest.

memoryHook.text: Deprive - a villain completely deprived of any moral goodness.

entries.etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin deprecat- 'prayed against', from the verb deprecari.
entries.exampleSentence: Always over-modest, he deprecated his contribution to the local charity.

memoryHook.text: Appreciate (the opposite) - to heavily express disapproval of something, the exact opposite of appreciating it.

entries.etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin derelictus 'forsaken', past participle of derelinquere.
entries.exampleSentence: Even though it was dangerous, the children enjoyed going to the deserted lot and playing in the derelict house.

memoryHook.text: Dairy licked - the abandoned ice cream factory is so ruined that stray dogs have licked all the rusted dairy vats.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin deridere 'scoff at'.
entries.exampleSentence: The bullies derided the foreign student’s accent.

memoryHook.text: The ride - ruthlessly mocking the kid who is too scared to go on the roller coaster ride.

entries.definition: Taken directly from a source, unoriginal
entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from French dérivatif, -ive, from Latin derivare 'draw off'.
entries.exampleSentence: She was bored by his music because she felt that it was derivative and that she had heard it before.

entries.definition: To violate the sacredness of a thing or place
entries.etymology: Late 17th century: from de- (expressing reversal) + consecrate.
entries.exampleSentence: They feared that the construction of a golf course would desecrate the preserved wilderness.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (reverse) + "sacr" (sacred) = to deliberately ruin something holy.

entries.etymology: Late 16th century: from Latin desiccat- 'made thoroughly dry', from the verb desiccare.
entries.exampleSentence: The skin of the desiccated mummy looked like old paper.

memoryHook.text: Desert - completely dried out and lifeless, exactly like a desert.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin desolatus 'abandoned', from the verb desolare.
entries.exampleSentence: She found the desolate landscape quite a contrast to the hustle and bustle of the overcrowded city.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (completely) + "sol" (alone) = completely left alone, empty, and barren.

entries.etymology: Late 17th century: from Latin despondent- 'giving up', from the verb despondere.
entries.exampleSentence: Having failed the first math test, the despondent child saw no use in studying for the next and failed that one too.

memoryHook.text: Respond - someone so hopelessly depressed they won't even respond to your texts.

entries.definition: One who has total power and rules brutally
entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from French despote, from Greek despotēs 'master, absolute ruler'.
entries.exampleSentence: The despot issued a death sentence for anyone who disobeyed his laws.

memoryHook.text: Death pot - a cruel, absolute dictator who throws his enemies into a boiling death pot.

entries.etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin destitutus 'forsaken', past participle of destituere.
entries.exampleSentence: The hurricane destroyed many homes and left many families destitute.

memoryHook.text: Deserted - someone who has been entirely deserted by wealth, leaving them in extreme poverty.

entries.etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin deterrere, from de- 'away from' + terrere 'frighten'.
entries.exampleSentence: Bob’s description of scary snakes couldn’t deter Marcia from traveling in the rainforests.

entries.etymology: From Latin devius ‘off the road,’ hence roundabout or dishonest.
entries.exampleSentence: Not wanting to be punished, the devious girl blamed the broken vase on the cat.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (away) + "via" (way) = wandering away from the straight, honest path to be sneaky.

entries.etymology: Greek dialektos ‘discourse,’ via Latin dialectus.
entries.exampleSentence: In the country’s remote, mountainous regions, the inhabitants spoke a dialect that the country’s other inhabitants had difficulty understanding.

entries.etymology: Greek diaphanēs ‘showing through,’ from dia- + phainein ‘show.’
entries.exampleSentence: Sunlight poured in through the diaphanous curtains, brightening the room.

memoryHook.text: "dia-" (through) + "phan" (show) = fabric so light and sheer that light shows right through it.

entries.etymology: Greek didaktikos ‘skilled in teaching,’ from didáskein ‘teach.’

senses.exampleSentence: She wrote up a didactic document showing new employees how to handle the company’s customers.
His didactic style of teaching made it seem like he wanted to persuade his students not to understand history fully, but to understand it from only one point of view.

memoryHook.text: Dictator - a teacher who constantly lectures you on morals like a strict dictator.

entries.exampleSentence: She wrote up a didactic document showing new employees how to handle the company’s customers.

entries.etymology: Latin diffidere ‘to mistrust,’ hence lacking self-confidence.
entries.exampleSentence: While eating dinner with the adults, the diffident youth did not speak for fear of seeming presumptuous.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (not) + "fid" (trust) = lacking trust in your own abilities; highly shy and unconfident.

entries.etymology: Latin diffundere ‘pour out,’ from dis- + fundere ‘pour.’

senses.definition: To scatter, thin out, break up
senses.exampleSentence: He diffused the tension in the room by making a joke.
In her writings, she tried unsuccessfully to make others understand her diffuse thoughts.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "fundere" (pour) = poured apart, spreading out thinly over a wide area.

entries.definition: To scatter, thin out, break up
entries.exampleSentence: He diffused the tension in the room by making a joke.

entries.etymology: Late Latin dilatorius ‘tending to delay,’ from differre ‘put off.’
entries.exampleSentence: The general’s dilatory strategy enabled the enemy to regroup.

memoryHook.text: Delay story - the lawyer told a long, pointless delay story just to waste time in court.

entries.definition: Showing care in doing one’s work
entries.etymology: Latin diligere ‘to love, esteem,’ extended to careful effort.
entries.exampleSentence: The diligent researcher made sure to check her measurements multiple times.

entries.etymology: Latin deminuere ‘lessen,’ via diminutivus ‘small-sized.’

memoryHook.text: "min" (small) = extremely or unusually small.

entries.definition: A mournful song, especially for a funeral

entries.etymology: Latin dis- ‘apart’ + affectus ‘disposed,’ reversal of favorable feeling.

entries.definition: To deny knowledge of or responsibility for

entries.etymology: Latin discernere ‘separate apart,’ hence distinguish.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (un) + "close" = to un-close the vault and reveal a secret.

entries.definition: Not agreeing, not in harmony with

entries.definition: Difference, failure of things to correspond
entries.etymology: Latin discrepantia ‘discord,’ from discrepare ‘sound differently.’

entries.definition: The quality of being reserved in speech or action; good.
entries.etymology: Latin discretio ‘separation, discernment,’ from discernere.

entries.etymology: Latin discursus ‘running about,’ from dis- + currere ‘run.’

entries.etymology: Latin disdēns ‘feeling aversion,’ from dis- + dignārī ‘deem worthy.’

senses.definition: To scorn, hold in low esteem

memoryHook.text: This stain - looking with absolute snobby disgust at "this stain" on your brand new shirt.

entries.definition: To scorn, hold in low esteem
entries.exampleSentence: Insecure about their jobs, the older employees disdained the recently hired ones, who were young and capable.

entries.etymology: dis- + gruntled (obsolete ‘content’); playful formation modeled on earlier senses.
entries.exampleSentence: The child believed that his parents had unjustly grounded him, and remained disgruntled for a week.

memoryHook.text: Grunt - an angry, dissatisfied worker walking away just grunting under his breath.

entries.definition: Feeling a loss of spirit or morale
entries.etymology: dis- + hearten; meaning loss of courage.
entries.exampleSentence: The team was disheartened after losing in the finals of the tournament.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (remove) + "heart" = having your courage or spirit entirely taken away.

entries.definition: To criticize or speak ill of

entries.exampleSentence: The saleswoman disparaged the competitor’s products to persuade her customers to buy what she was selling.

memoryHook.text: Spar - to verbally spar with someone just to belittle and insult their worth.

entries.definition: Sharply differing, containing sharply contrasting elements
entries.etymology: Latin disparātus ‘separated,’ from dis- + parāre ‘prepare as distinct.’
entries.exampleSentence: Having widely varying interests, the students had disparate responses toward the novel.

memoryHook.text: Separate - two things that are so fundamentally different they must be kept totally separate.

entries.definition: To send off to accomplish a duty
entries.etymology: Italian dispacciare or Spanish despachar ‘send off hastily.’
entries.exampleSentence: The carpenter dispatched his assistant to fetch wood.

entries.etymology: Latin dispellere ‘drive apart,’ from dis- + pellere ‘drive.’
entries.exampleSentence: She entered the office as usual on Monday, dispelling the rumor that she had been fired.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "pel" (drive) = to drive away a rumor or bad feeling.

entries.etymology: Latin dispersus ‘scattered,’ past participle of dispergere.
entries.exampleSentence: When the rain began to pour, the crowd at the baseball game quickly dispersed.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "spars" (scatter) = to scatter the crowd in different directions.

entries.definition: A state of being held in low regard
entries.etymology: French désréputation; Latin repute replaced by disrepute pattern.
entries.exampleSentence: The officer fell into disrepute after it was learned that he had disobeyed the orders he had given to his own soldiers.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (not) + "repute" (reputation) = held in extremely low esteem by the public.

entries.etymology: Middle French dissembler ‘not resemble,’ mask true intentions.
entries.exampleSentence: Not wanting to appear heartlessly greedy, she dissembled and hid her intention to sell her ailing father’s stamp collection.

memoryHook.text: Disassemble - to disassemble your true face and hide behind a fake, deceptive one.

entries.etymology: Latin dissemināre ‘scatter seed,’ from dis- + semen ‘seed.’
entries.exampleSentence: The politician disseminated his ideas across the town before the election.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "semin" (seed) = to scatter information widely, just like a farmer planting seeds.

entries.etymology: Latin dissentīre ‘feel differently,’ from dis- + sentīre ‘feel.’

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "sent" (feel) = to feel apart from the group; to officially disagree.

entries.exampleSentence: The principal argued that the child should repeat the fourth grade, but the unhappy parents dissented.

entries.etymology: Latin dissipāre ‘scatter,’ from dis- + supāre ‘to throw.’

entries.exampleSentence: The sun finally came out and dissipated the haze.

entries.etymology: Latin dissonantia ‘disagreement in sound,’ from dis- + sonāre ‘sound.’
entries.exampleSentence: Though the president of the company often spoke of the company as reliant solely upon its workers, her decision to increase her own salary rather than reward her employees revealed a striking dissonance between her alleged beliefs and her actions.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "son" (sound) = musical notes that clash aggressively and don't belong together.

entries.definition: To persuade someone not to do something
entries.etymology: Latin dissuādēre ‘advise against,’ from dis- + suādēre ‘urge.’
entries.exampleSentence: Worried that he would catch a cold, she tried to dissuade him from going out on winter nights.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (against) + "suadere" (persuade) = to heavily persuade someone not to do something.

entries.etymology: Latin distendere ‘stretch apart,’ from dis- + tendere ‘stretch.’
entries.exampleSentence: Years of drinking beer caused his stomach to distend.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "tend" (stretch) = to stretch outwards, usually used for medical swelling.

entries.etymology: Middle English didderen ‘tremble’; modern sense of indecision.

memoryHook.text: Dizzy - spinning around dizzy, completely paralyzed and unable to make a decision.

entries.etymology: Latin dīvīnus ‘of a god,’ from deus ‘god.’ Multiple senses converged in English.
entries.exampleSentence: Terribly fond of desserts, she found the rich chocolate cake to be divine.

memoryHook.text: Vine - predicting the future by reading the tangled growth of a magic vine.

entries.etymology: Latin dīvīs-, past stem of dīvidere ‘divide.’
entries.exampleSentence: Her divisive tactics turned her two friends against each other.

entries.etymology: Latin dīvulgāre ‘publish widely,’ from dis- + vulgus ‘common people.’
entries.exampleSentence: Pressured by the press, the government finally divulged the previously unknown information.

memoryHook.text: Bulge - the secret is bulging out of your pockets until you finally crack and reveal it.

entries.etymology: Latin docilis ‘teachable,’ from docēre ‘teach.’
entries.exampleSentence: She successfully taught the docile puppy several tricks.

memoryHook.text: "doc" (teach) = easily taught and completely submissive to handling.

entries.definition: Aggressively and arrogantly certain about unproved.
entries.etymology: Greek dogmatikos ‘opinionated,’ from dogma ‘opinion, decree.’

memoryHook.text: Dog - a dog guarding its bone is stubbornly attached to it, just like someone stubbornly attached to their unproven opinion.

entries.etymology: Latin dormīre ‘sleep,’ present participle dormiens.

memoryHook.text: "dorm" (sleep) = currently sleeping or temporarily inactive (like a volcano).

entries.etymology: Latin dūrus ‘hard’ influenced Gaelic and Middle English senses.

memoryHook.text: Sour - having a sour, gloomy, and extremely severe facial expression.

entries.etymology: Latin dubius ‘wavering,’ from duo ‘two’ minds.
entries.exampleSentence: Suspicious that he was only trying to get a raise, she found his praise dubious.

memoryHook.text: "dub" (doubt) = a claim filled with hesitation and serious doubt.

entries.etymology: Latin duplicitās ‘doubleness,’ from duplex ‘twofold.’

memoryHook.text: "du" (two) + "plic" (fold) = being two-faced or highly deceitful.

entries.etymology: Latin dūrus ‘hard’ + neuter abstract formation via Old French duress.

entries.etymology: Greek dunamikos ‘powerful,’ from dunamis ‘power.’

entries.etymology: Latin ēbullīre ‘bubble out,’ from e- + bullīre ‘boil.’

entries.definition: Consisting of a diverse variety of elements
entries.etymology: Greek eklektikos ‘selective,’ from eklegein ‘pick out.’

memoryHook.text: Elect - electing to take the best individual pieces from a wide variety of different styles.

entries.etymology: Greek ekstasis ‘standing outside oneself,’ intense displacement.

entries.etymology: Latin ēdictum ‘thing proclaimed,’ neuter past participle of ēdīcere.

entries.definition: To wipe out, obliterate, rub away

entries.etymology: Latin ēgregius ‘standing out from the flock,’ from ex- + grex ‘herd.’

entries.etymology: Latin ēlabōrāre ‘work out,’ later senses of ornament and complexity.

entries.definition: A speech given in honor of a dead person

entries.exampleSentence: My mom embellished the living room by adding lace curtains.

entries.definition: To correct or revise a written text

entries.exampleSentence: Mr. Phillips is such an eminent scholar that every professor on campus has come to hear him lecture.

entries.exampleSentence: The scientist gathered empirical data on the growth rate of dandelions by studying the dandelions behind his house.

entries.etymology: Latin aemulārī ‘strive to equal,’ from aemulus ‘rivaling.’

entries.definition: To fill with love, fascinate, usually used in passive.

entries.definition: The audience’s demand for a repeat performance; also the.

entries.etymology: Old French enemistié; Latin inimīcitia ‘unfriendliness.’

entries.etymology: French ennui ‘annoyance, boredom,’ from Latin in odio ‘in hatred.’

entries.definition: To include as a necessary step
entries.etymology: Anglo-Norman entailer ‘cut in,’ restricting inheritance; extended to logical consequence.

entries.exampleSentence: She promised she’d love me forever, but her “forever” was only ephemeral: she left me after one week.
entries.etymology: Greek *ephemeros* ‘lasting a day,’ from *epi-* ‘upon’ + *hemera* ‘day.’

entries.definition: Relating to or contained in letters

entries.etymology: Latin *epistolaris*, from Greek *epistolē* ‘letter, message.’

entries.exampleSentence: Even though he had just been fired, Mr. Simms showed great equanimity by neatly packing up his desk and wishing everyone in the office well.

entries.exampleSentence: His intentions were so equivocal that I didn’t know whether he was being chivalrous or sleazy.

entries.exampleSentence: My Latin teacher is such an erudite scholar that he has translated some of the most difficult and abstruse ancient poetry.
entries.etymology: Latin *eruditus* ‘well instructed,’ from *e-* ‘out’ + *rudis* ‘raw, untrained.’

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "rud" (raw/rude) = trained completely out of a raw, ignorant state into profound scholarly knowledge.

entries.exampleSentence: George hates the color green so much that he eschews all green food.
entries.etymology: Middle English from Old French *eschiver*, akin to German *scheuen* ‘to shun, dread.’

memoryHook.text: Ah - choo!-you sneeze, and I deliberately avoid you to escape the germs.

entries.definition: Understood by only a select few

entries.etymology: Greek *esōterikos* ‘inner,’ comparative of *esō* ‘within’ (opposed to exoteric).

memoryHook.text: Isolated - knowledge that is so highly isolated that only a tiny group of experts actually understand it.

entries.definition: To take up as a cause, support

entries.etymology: Middle English from Old French *espouser* ‘to marry,’ from Latin *sponsus* ‘betrothed’; figuratively to take up a cause.

entries.exampleSentence: In her flowing silk gown and lace veil, the bride looked ethereal.
entries.etymology: Latin *aetherius*, from Greek *aithēr* ‘upper bright air.’

entries.definition: The history of words, their origin and development

entries.etymology: Greek *etumologia*, from *etumon* ‘true sense’ + *logos* ‘account’—the study of a word’s true origin.

entries.exampleSentence: I was euphoric when I found out that my sister had given birth to twins.

memoryHook.text: "eu-" (good) + "pherein" (bear) = bearing a feeling of intense, overwhelmingly good happiness.

entries.exampleSentence: My joy at getting promoted was evanescent because I discovered that I would have to work much longer hours in a less friendly office.

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "vanescere" (vanish) = fading rapidly out of sight; vanishing like vapor.

entries.exampleSentence: Christopher’s hand-wringing and nail-biting evince how nervous he is about the upcoming English test.

memoryHook.text: Evidence - to clearly show evidence or proof of a hidden feeling.

entries.exampleSentence: The gruesome and scary movie I saw last night exacerbated my fears of the dark.
entries.etymology: Latin *exacerbare*, from *ex-* ‘thoroughly’ + *acerbus* ‘harsh, bitter.’

memoryHook.text: Extra bad - making a bad situation extra bad by interfering.

entries.exampleSentence: Michael Jordan is the figure in basketball we exalt the most.
entries.etymology: Latin *exaltare* ‘to raise high,’ from *ex-* ‘up’ + *altus* ‘high.’

memoryHook.text: Exhale - exhaling in pure awe as you praise and elevate a hero to a high rank.

entries.exampleSentence: George’s endless complaints exasperated his roomate.
entries.etymology: Latin *exasperare* ‘to roughen,’ from *ex-* + *asper* ‘rough.’

memoryHook.text: Ex-aspirin - you have a splitting headache and realize you are out of aspirin, which thoroughly annoys and frustrates you.

entries.definition: To dig out of the ground and remove
entries.exampleSentence: The pharaoh’s treasures were excavated by archeologists in Egypt.
entries.etymology: Latin *excavare*, from *ex-* ‘out’ + *cavus* ‘hollow.’

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "cav" (cave) = to hollow out a cave or systematically dig historical artifacts out of the ground.

entries.definition: To free from guilt or blame, exonerate
entries.exampleSentence: My discovery of the ring behind the dresser exculpated me from the charge of having stolen it.
entries.etymology: Latin *exculpare*, from *ex-* ‘from’ + *culpa* ‘blame.’

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "culp" (blame) = to officially take the blame off someone's record.

entries.exampleSentence: After taking an excursion to the Bronx Zoo, I dreamed about pandas and monkeys.
entries.etymology: Latin *excursio* ‘a running out,’ from *excurrere* ‘to run forth.’

entries.exampleSentence: Her pudding is so execrable that it makes me sick.
entries.etymology: Latin *execrabilis*, from *execrari* ‘to curse,’ *ex-* + *sacer* ‘sacred’ (invoke a curse).

memoryHook.text: Excrement - a movie so utterly foul and detestable it belongs in the sewer with the excrement.

entries.exampleSentence: Henry exhorted his colleagues to join him in protesting against the university’s hiring policies.
entries.etymology: Latin *exhortari* ‘to encourage strongly,’ from *ex-* + *hortari* ‘to urge.’

memoryHook.text: Exhaust - cheering and urging your team on so intensely from the sidelines that you exhaust yourself.

entries.exampleSentence: The patient has an exigent need for medication, or else he will lose his sight.
entries.etymology: Latin *exigens*, present participle of *exigere* ‘to demand,’ literally ‘drive out.’

memoryHook.text: Exit gent - the building is currently on fire, so it is highly urgent and demanding that the gentleman exits immediately.

entries.definition: To free from guilt or blame, exculpate
entries.exampleSentence: The true thief’s confession exonerated the man who had been held in custody for the crime.
entries.etymology: Latin *exonerare* ‘to unload,’ from *ex-* + *onus* ‘burden’—remove the burden of guilt.

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "onus" (burden) = taking the heavy burden of guilt permanently off someone's shoulders.

entries.exampleSentence: Her exorbitant praise made me blush and squirm in my seat.
entries.etymology: Late Latin *exorbitans* ‘going off track,’ from *ex-* + *orbita* ‘wheel track.’

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "orbit" = a price tag so insanely high it has flown completely out of orbit.

entries.exampleSentence: In his bid for reelection, the governor made an expedient move by tabling all controversial legislation.
entries.etymology: Latin *expedire* ‘to free the feet,’ from *ex-* + *pes* ‘foot’—set free, dispatch, then advantageous.

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "ped" (foot) = originally meaning to free a foot from a trap; doing what is most convenient and practical to solve a problem…

entries.exampleSentence: To expiate my selfishness, I gave all my profits to charity.
entries.etymology: Latin *expiare*, from *ex-* ‘thoroughly’ + *piare* ‘to appease’ (*pius* ‘pious’).

memoryHook.text: Expiration - the pirate must do good deeds to make amends for his crimes before his expiration date.

entries.exampleSentence: Fearful of an IRS investigation, Paul tried to expunge all incriminating evidence from his tax files.
entries.etymology: Latin *expungere* ‘to prick out’ (cancel marks), from *ex-* + *pungere* ‘to prick.’

memoryHook.text: Sponge - using a massive sponge to completely wipe away and erase a criminal record.

entries.definition: To remove offensive or incorrect parts, usually of a book
entries.exampleSentence: The history editors expurgated from the text all disparaging and inflammatory comments about the Republican Party.
entries.etymology: Latin *expurgare* ‘to cleanse,’ from *ex-* + *purgare* ‘purify.’

memoryHook.text: Purge - the school board deciding to purge a classic book of all its offensive content before assigning it.

entries.exampleSentence: My mother’s extant love letters to my father are in the attic trunk.
entries.etymology: Latin *extans*, present participle of *exstare* ‘to stand out, exist,’ from *ex-* + *stare* ‘stand.’

memoryHook.text: X-tent - the ancient explorer's tent still miraculously stands; it currently exists and survived the storm.

entries.exampleSentence: Violet extolled the virtues of a vegetarian diet to her meatloving brother.
entries.etymology: Latin *extollere* ‘to lift up,’ from *ex-* + *tollere* ‘raise.’

memoryHook.text: Toll - paying the toll bridge operator with highly enthusiastic praise instead of actual money.

entries.exampleSentence: Personal political ambitions should always remain extraneous to legislative policy, but, unfortunately, they rarely are.
entries.etymology: Latin *extraneus* ‘external,’ from *extra* ‘outside.’

memoryHook.text: "extra" (outside) = coming from the outside and therefore entirely irrelevant to the core subject.

entries.exampleSentence: Instead of trying to mediate between my brother and sister, I extricated myself from the family tension entirely and left the house for the day.
entries.etymology: Latin *extricare*, from *ex-* + *tricae* ‘troubles, hindrances.’

memoryHook.text: Trick - finally figuring out the puzzle's trick to free yourself from the trap.

entries.exampleSentence: When she found out she won the literature prize, Mary exulted by dancing and singing through the school’s halls.
entries.etymology: Latin *exultare* ‘to leap up,’ from *ex-* + *saltare* ‘to leap.’

memoryHook.text: Vault-pole - vaulting high into the air out of sheer triumph and joy after winning gold.

entries.definition: To make up, invent (When I arrived an hour late to.
entries.exampleSentence: Despite my smiling façade, I am feeling melancholy.
entries.etymology: Latin *fabricare* ‘to construct,’ from *faber* ‘smith, maker.’
Latin *facilis* ‘easy,’ from *facere* ‘to do’—done with ease, hence (disparagingly) too easy.

senses.exampleSentence: This game is so facile that even a four-yearold can master it.

senses.definition: Superficial, achieved with minimal thought or care, insincere
senses.exampleSentence: The business was in such shambles that any solution seemed facile at best; nothing could really helpit in the long-run.

memoryHook.text: "fac" (do/make) = an argument that is so easy to make that it is superficial and entirely lacks depth.

entries.exampleSentence: This game is so facile that even a four-yearold can master it.
Emily offered me cigarettes on the fallacious assumption that I smoked.
entries.etymology: Latin *fallaciosus*, from *fallere* ‘to deceive.’

memoryHook.text: "fall" (deceive) = based on a false, highly deceitful premise.

entries.definition: Meticulous, demanding, having high and often.
entries.exampleSentence: Mark is so fastidious that he is never able to finish a project because it always seems imperfect to him.
entries.etymology: Latin *fastidiosus* ‘disdainful,’ from *fastidium* ‘loathing, squeamishness.’

memoryHook.text: Fast tidier - obsessing over every tiny detail; nothing sloppy gets past.

entries.exampleSentence: I cannot fathom why you like that crabby and mean-spirited neighbor of ours.
entries.etymology: Old English *fæthm* ‘span of the outstretched arms,’ used of measuring depth.

memoryHook.text: Originally a nautical unit of measurement for water depth (about 6 feet). To fathom a problem is to successfully reach the very bottom of…

entries.exampleSentence: He considers himself a serious poet, but in truth, he only writes fatuous limericks.
entries.etymology: Latin *fatuus* ‘foolish,’ akin to *faeri* ‘to speak’ as babble.

memoryHook.text: Tattoo us - two drunk, incredibly silly friends shouting "Tattoo us!" at 3 AM; a foolish, pointless idea.

entries.exampleSentence: The fecund tree bore enough apples to last us through the entire season.
entries.etymology: Latin *fecundus* ‘fruitful,’ from *fe-/*fetus* ‘offspring.’

memoryHook.text: Feces - putting cow feces (manure) on the soil makes it highly fertile and incredibly productive.

entries.etymology: Latin *felix* ‘happy, fertile’ + *-ous*; suited to the occasion.

senses.exampleSentence: While his comments were idiotic and rambling, mine were felicitous and helpful.
I spent a felicitous afternoon visiting old friends.

memoryHook.text: Fleece - finding a warm fleece jacket in your trunk right as a blizzard hits is a highly fortunate, well-timed event.

entries.exampleSentence: While his comments were idiotic and rambling, mine were felicitous and helpful.
That beast looks so feral that I would fear being alone with it.
entries.etymology: Latin *fera* ‘wild beast,’ from *ferus* ‘wild.’

memoryHook.text: Fur all - a stray animal covered in fur all over, acting completely wild and untamed.

entries.exampleSentence: The fervent protestors chained themselves to the building and shouted all night long.
entries.etymology: Latin *fervens* ‘boiling,’ present participle of *fervere* ‘to boil, glow.’

memoryHook.text: Fever - having a burning, passionate fever for a political cause.

entries.exampleSentence: I can tell from the fetid smell in your refrigerator that your milk has spoiled.
entries.etymology: Latin *fetidus* ‘stinking,’ from *fetere* ‘to stink.’

memoryHook.text: Feet - a swamp smelling as horrific as a rotting pair of unwashed feet.

entries.exampleSentence: The dog was fettered to the parking meter.
entries.etymology: Old English *feter* ‘chain,’ from Proto-Germanic *fetru*.

memoryHook.text: Feet - putting heavy iron chains on a prisoner's feet to severely restrict them.

entries.exampleSentence: In Greek dramas, the fickle gods help Achilles one day, and then harm him the next.
entries.etymology: Middle English *fikel* ‘deceitful,’ from Old English *ficel* ‘pertaining to fraud.’

memoryHook.text: Pickle - you can't decide if you want a sweet or a sour pickle because your tastes change constantly and unpredictably.

entries.exampleSentence: Guard dogs are known for the great fidelity they show toward their masters.
entries.etymology: Latin *fidelitas*, from *fides* ‘faith, trust.’

memoryHook.text: "fid" (trust/faith) = strict faithfulness and unwavering loyalty.

entries.exampleSentence: Using figurative language, Jane likened the storm to an angry bull.
entries.etymology: Middle English from Late Latin *figurativus*, from Latin *figura* ‘shape, figure.’

entries.exampleSentence: Whenever I read an Agatha Christie mystery novel, I am always flabbergasted when I learn the identity of the murderer.
entries.etymology: Colloquial 18th-c. British coinage; origin uncertain (perhaps playful blend).

entries.exampleSentence: If a plant is not watered enough, its leaves become droopy and flaccid.
entries.etymology: Latin *flaccidus*, from *flaccus* ‘flabby, hanging loose.’

memoryHook.text: Flat - a tire that is completely flat, limp, and entirely lacking firmness.

entries.exampleSentence: The judge’s decision to set the man free simply because that man was his brother was a flagrant abuse of power.
entries.etymology: Latin *flagrans* ‘blazing,’ present participle of *flagrare* ‘to burn.’

memoryHook.text: Flag - a foul on the basketball court so obviously bad and intentional the referee throws a giant penalty flag.

entries.exampleSentence: The writer’s florid prose belongs on a sentimental Hallmark card.
entries.etymology: Latin *floridus* ‘flowery,’ from *flos* ‘flower.’

memoryHook.text: "flor" (flower) = writing that is heavily decorated and excessively flowery.

entries.exampleSentence: I flouted the school’s dress code by wearing a tie-dyed tank top and a pair of cut-off jeans.
entries.etymology: Perhaps from Middle Dutch *fluiten* ‘to whistle at’ in mockery; influenced by *flout* ‘jest.’

memoryHook.text: Fly out - to openly mock and break the rules, then fly out of the country to avoid arrest.

entries.exampleSentence: Inspector Wilkens foiled the thieves by locking them in the bank along with their stolen money.
entries.etymology: From obsolete *foil* ‘defeat’ (as in hunting), perhaps from *full* ‘to tread’; distinct from metal *foil.*

memoryHook.text: Aluminum foil - wrapping the villain's evil plans in heavy aluminum foil to completely block and ruin them.

entries.exampleSentence: When we got lost on our hiking trip, we foraged for berries and nuts in order to survive.
entries.etymology: Old French *fourrage*, from *fuerre* ‘straw, fodder,’ Frankish *fodr* ‘food.’

entries.exampleSentence: The doctor showed great forbearance in calming down the angry patient who shouted insults at him.
entries.etymology: From *forbear* + *-ance*; Old English *forberan* ‘to endure.’

memoryHook.text: Bear - having the immense patience and self-control not to shoot the bear when it crosses your path.

entries.exampleSentence: I forestalled the cold I was getting by taking plenty of vitamin C pills and wearing a scarf.
entries.etymology: Middle English *forestallen* ‘to intercept goods before market,’ from *fore-* + *stall* ‘marketplace.’

memoryHook.text: Before stall - placing a heavy stall in the road before the enemy arrives to entirely prevent their advance.

entries.exampleSentence: Even though I had the flu, my family decided to go skiing for the weekend and leave me home alone, feeling feverish and forlorn.
entries.etymology: Old English *forloren* ‘lost, past participle of *forleosan* ‘lose utterly.’

memoryHook.text: Torn - your heart is totally torn because you are pitifully sad, lonely, and abandoned.

entries.exampleSentence: My New Year’s resolution is to forsake smoking and drinking.
entries.etymology: Old English *forsacan* ‘to renounce,’ from *for-* ‘completely’ + *sacan* ‘to dispute, deny.’

entries.exampleSentence: Achilles’ fortitude in battle is legendary.
entries.etymology: Latin *fortitudo*, from *fortis* ‘strong, brave.’

memoryHook.text: "fort" (strong) = immense mental strength and courage while enduring deep pain.

entries.definition: Happening by chance, often lucky or fortunate
entries.exampleSentence: After looking for Manuel and not finding him at home, Harriet had a fortuitous encounter with him at the post office.
entries.etymology: Latin *fortuitus* ‘happening by chance,’ from *forte* ‘by chance,’ from * fors* ‘luck.’

memoryHook.text: "fortun" (chance) = something great happening by a lucky, happy accident rather than design.

entries.definition: A medium for lecture or discussion
entries.exampleSentence: Some radio talk-shows provide a good forum for political debate.
entries.etymology: Latin *forum* ‘open square, public place,’ related to *foris* ‘outside.’

entries.exampleSentence: To foster good health in the city, the mayor started a “Get out and exercise!” campaign.
entries.etymology: Old English *fostrian* ‘to feed, nourish,’ from *fostor* ‘food, nourishment.’

entries.exampleSentence: Although the child insisted he wasn’t tired, his fractious behavior—especially his decision to crush his cheese and crackers all over the floor—convinced everyone present that it was time to put him to bed.
entries.etymology: From *fraction* in sense of discord; Latin *frangere* ‘to break’ + *-ous.’

memoryHook.text: "fract" (break) = a group that is easily broken into arguments; highly irritable and unruly.

entries.definition: (usually used with “with”) filled or accompanied with
entries.exampleSentence: Her glances in his direction were fraught with meaning, though precisely what meaning remained unclear.
entries.etymology: Middle Dutch *vracht* ‘load’; English sense ‘laden (with danger)’ from maritime cargo.

memoryHook.text: Caught - a tense situation where you are constantly worried you will be caught because the room is filled with danger.

entries.exampleSentence: In the hours between night and morning, the frenetic pace of city life slows to a lull.
entries.etymology: Old French *frenetique*, from Greek *phrenitis* ‘inflammation of the brain,’ from *phrēn* ‘mind.’

memoryHook.text: Frenzy - moving with wild, incredibly fast, frantic energy.

entries.exampleSentence: Someday, all that anxiety about whether your zit will disappear before the prom will seem totally frivolous.
entries.etymology: Latin *frivolus* ‘silly, empty,’ perhaps from *friare* ‘to crumble.’

memoryHook.text: Free volley - playing a completely meaningless, silly free volley in tennis instead of focusing on the actual match.

entries.exampleSentence: Richard is so frugal that his diet consists almost exclusively of catfish and chicken liver—the two most inexpensive foods in the store.
entries.etymology: Latin *frugalis*, from *frux* ‘fruit, value’—worthy of the fruit of one’s labor.

memoryHook.text: Fruit girl - the girl who only eats free fruit from the trees to save money because she is highly economical.

entries.exampleSentence: Jane’s placement of her drugs in her sock drawer was not as furtive as she thought, as the sock drawer is the first place most parents look.
entries.etymology: French *furtif*, from Latin *furtivus*, from *fur* ‘thief.’

memoryHook.text: Fur thief - the fur thief moves quietly and secretly through the mansion to avoid being caught.

entries.exampleSentence: Mrs. Watson has poor taste and covers every object in her house with a garish gold lamé.
entries.etymology: Perhaps from obsolete *gair* ‘to stare’; influenced by *yellow* folk etymology; uncertain.

memoryHook.text: Garnish - piling way too much cheap, brightly colored garnish on a plate, making the whole meal look tacky.

entries.exampleSentence: Some talk show hosts are so garrulous that their guests can’t get a word in edgewise.
entries.etymology: Latin *garrulus* ‘chattering,’ from *garrire* ‘to chatter.’

memoryHook.text: Gary rules - Gary never shuts up about how much he rules; an excessively talkative guy.

entries.exampleSentence: Although he’s been known to behave like a real jerk, I would say that my brother is an overall genial guy.
entries.etymology: Latin *genialis* ‘festive, pertaining to birth (*genius* spirit),’ from *genius* ‘attendant spirit.’

memoryHook.text: "gen" (nature/born) = having a naturally friendly, warm, and cheerful disposition.

entries.exampleSentence: Ada’s fried chicken tastes so divine, I don’t know how anyone can call gluttony a sin.
entries.etymology: Old French *glotonie*, from Latin *glutto* ‘glutton.’

entries.definition: To urge, spur, incite to action
entries.exampleSentence: Jim may think he’s not going to fight Billy, but Billy will goad Jim on with insults until he throws a punch.
entries.etymology: Old English *gād* ‘pointed stick,’ from Germanic *gaitō* ‘arrow, dart.’

memoryHook.text: Goat - poking a stubborn goat repeatedly with a sharp stick to provoke it to move.

entries.definition: Someone fond of eating and drinking
entries.exampleSentence: My parents, who used to eat little more than crackers and salad, have become real gourmands in their old age.
entries.etymology: French *gourmand* ‘glutton,’ from Old French *gourmer* ‘to swallow greedily.’

memoryHook.text: Gourmet man - a man who loves eating rich, gourmet food in excessive amounts.
